---
title: "Blog"
title-block-banner: false
---

Today I want to talk about an important article published in The American Statistician, titled “Moving to a World Beyond ‘p < 0.05’.” Co-authored by a group of leading statisticians, this piece presents argument: it is time to abandon the concept of “statistical significance” and rethink how we approach statistical inference.

For decades, researchers across disciplines have relied heavily on the p-value threshold of 0.05 to determine whether a result is “statistically significant.” While this practice was once useful as a simple rule of thumb, the article argues that the continued use of the “statistical significance” label now does more harm than good. The authors contend that dichotomizing results into “significant” and “non-significant” oversimplifies the complexity of data and promotes binary thinking. Rather than abandoning p-values altogether, they call for a shift in how we interpret them—moving beyond arbitrary thresholds and toward a deeper engagement with effect sizes, uncertainty, and context. This change would improve the quality of scientific discourse and encourage researchers to think more critically about their results. Embracing the uncertainty inherent in real data, rather than suppressing it, allows for a more nuanced and honest understanding of scientific evidence.

As a young researcher, I find this call especially resonant. Our generation has been trained from the start to view p-values as a central element of statistical thinking. From homework assignments to academic research, we were taught to consider “p < 0.05” as a kind of success criterion. Once we enter academia, the peer review system further reinforces this habit. Reviewers often look for “significant” results and tend to undervalue findings that are not, regardless of their real-world implications. This deep-rooted dependence on statistical significance is structural—and changing it will not be easy.

But change must begin somewhere. As early-career researchers, we are in a unique position to help lead this shift. Even though moving beyond “statistically significant” may cause discomfort and resistance—especially in systems so deeply built around p-values—it is a necessary step toward better science.
